Observation Details: Audio recorded. Suspicious for hairy Woodpecker. Much less squeaky than downy and was almost immediately after I played a hairy woodpecker call. Tried to locate but couldn’t. Heard another very similar call (not recorded) that definitely was not a downy. In the general area found 2 downy and a third Dryobates sp. that as it took off showed clean white outer tail feathers. Didn’t get to see the front of the bird unfortunately so couldn’t see the beak. I’m not comfortable marking HAWO based on my visual observation but if a more experienced birder can attest to the call being hairy I will mark it as such.  Edit: confirmed by both Tim H. and Andy K. as DOWO

Media notes: Two Blue-gray Gnatchers were mobbing both adult Downy Woodpecker which then drew in many other species. I’m assuming the gnatcatchers had a nest nearby.

Not sure if there was perhaps another threat but I didn’t hear any other predator.  A second nest on the main pathway, past the visitor center, has a possible hybrid female. I have changed the report and listed her as woodpecker sp. The male appears to be a Downy.

Observation Details: I recorded some of its drilling sound. It was close by--one or two lots down--when I started. Suddenly it sounded like another drilling in a tree further away took over immediately when the nearly session ended; there was not the customary pause between sessions, nor the time for a bird to fly from here to there. From that point on, the drilling was further away and may not have recorded as distinctly. The nearby drilling never resumed. After a while, the drilling sound moved yet further away; this time there was a long enough pause for the bird to fly the distance. For this reason, I think there were two woodpeckers.

An adult female foraging; adult males disputing territory, calling A foraging male was approached by a second, which began making the "pik" call over and over in close succession.  When the first male left the scene, the territorial male left longer time between each "pik" call until it just stopped altogether. The recording is of this ending.  Audio:

Observation Details: Continuing. I focused this outing on the Downy woodpecker only. I went straight to the cairn in the middle of the creek that Ryan placed (thx!), then walked south in the creek to just beyond the workstation. Then walked back to the cairn. Many LBWO sightings. Then walked south again. Then back to the cairn. It was my 3rd trip walking south is when I found it (9:16AM). I heard a whinny much higher pitch than LBWO, and unlike HAWO whinny. Located it fairly low in low (dead) cottonwood branches overhanging the creek bed immediately east from a pair of picnic tables in the workstation area.       It whinnied once again and I managed to get a recording/video.  I believe this bird is chaseable. It remained in this area over the creek for 15 minutes.  Smaller than LBWO and HAWO. White stripe on back. Flight unlike LBWO and HAWO (to me it seemed like a WBNU flight). Male. White belly and very small bill.  photos.

Media notes: This audio recording captured the single-note calls of a female Downy (that I watched in binoculars) on trees along a river. ("Pik" calls heard from female Downy high in tree, possibly heard at 00:14 and 00:21 seconds (both very faint) and heard very clearly at 00:29 seconds in the recording.) ... (This audio recording also includes the drumming of a Dryobates species. [I'm not sure whether Downy Woodpecker or Hairy Woodpeckers; both were active in this area at the time.] The drumming is heard at about 00:06 seconds and again at 00:34 seconds into the recording. ... Automobile noise also heard in the background.)
Observation Details: Saw and/or heard at least three individuals in the same area, on either side of river (observed from SE part of first bend in river). ... (Also heard occasional drumming from a Dryobates species, which I think was from Hairy Woodpeckers.) ... Eventually observed one individual calling from near top of dead tree just across river, then watched as a second individual flew just up the branch, making squeaky noises while abruptly shifting itself left, middle, right, with its bill pointed straight up. ... (Posted audio recording of a female Downy making "pik" calls [heard very faintly at about 00:29 seconds], with drumming [from Hairy Woodpecker, heard at 00:06 seconds and again at 00:39 seconds].)

Observation Details: Adult female was widening an opening in a House Wren box, continued work from yesterday. An adult male chased her away and she made this twittering sound. Every time the sound was heard I could see her tail shaking showing that she was the one who made the noise. Her bill did not open, nor did her throat seem to move.  One of  2 Tufted Titmice were interested in trying to get into the opening in the box and the male Downy Woodpecker chased it away.  Black-capped Chickadee also landed on and looked inside the opening.

Recorder: RecForge II with LG G7 ThinQ smartphone.
Media notes: An adult female downy woodpecker enlarging the hole in a House Wren box I had made with a 1 1/8" diameter hole.
Observation Details: Female Downy Woodpecker enlarging a 1 1/8 inch opening in a House Wren box I had made. Much white in the coverts. It was briefly chased away by a male Red-bellied Woodpecker, but it came back and continued working when the larger woodpecker flew away. It moved away to a nearby branch when a Blue Jay landed in a branch above it. It initially worked for 41 minutes, then flew away for 26 minutes, came back and worked again for 61 minutes. It then flew away for 30 minutes, then came back and worked on the opening for 31 minutes when it was chased away by a male Downy Woodpecker. The female kept trying to come back to the opening. The male chased her away several times while it pecked at the opening for less than one minute. The male left. 45 minutes later and the female came back and continued working on the opening for 30 minutes. The female flew away for 28 minutes, then worked on the opening for 9 minutes and was then able to get into the box, then began cleaning the inside and tossing out wood chips for 20 minutes and then flew out.

Media notes: Drumming from single individual, although the second dummer is heard once. Prior to recording, two individuals were counter-drumming. Counter-drumming can be heard in the background of ML220317091 (RBWO), one of the reasons I think this recording is from DOWO. I had heard RBWO in the area, but later saw two DOWO during a break in drumming. The two DOWO were together on the same snag, then one flew farther to the east. After that, counter-drumming resumed, with one drummer in the place I had seen the two together, the second drummer from farther east. I did not see either drummer, though. Drum pattern / rate is consistent with DOWO.

He also was performing very brief and fast drillings on the tree limb, which likely were a territorial drilling. Another Downy eventually responded from across the bay, and when it did he looked in that direction.

"Pik" note starts just a bit before second 2, possibly also at 5.5 and 8.3.  The main sonogram component of the first and loudest note is a blurry dark horizontal band between 6 and 7  kHz.  (Possible faint che-bek call of Least Flycatcher at the very beginning of the clip.)
Observation Details: In one sense, this is a simple report on juvenal plumage and adult male and female plumages of Downy Woodpecker, with photos.  But really this entry, along with the entry for Downy/Hairy Woodpecker below, is about the complexities of the seemingly fairly simple differentation between Downy and Hairy Woodpecker.  The occasion of this deeper dive into such complexities was an eBird report early on the same day of a (rare for Harris County) Hairy Woodpecker at ELMNS feeders.  (Details on this early report below, in Hairy/Downy entry.)  The problem is that key marks "usually" point the way to a correct ID.  But not always.  Several examples of complexities are brought forward in the entries here for Downy Woodpecker and Downy/Hairy Woodpecker.

Media notes: Drummer Boy
Observation Details: Small woodpecker with small bill.  Unmarked whitish flanks.  Foraging different than other woodpeckers due to the small size of the Downy Woodpecker.  Such foraging often includes the woodpecker clinging upside down on small branches.  Dark tips on white outer tail feathers, which Hairy Woodpecker also doesn't have that much of.  Calls are also different than HAWO and ATTW and are actually very close in sound to Ladder-backed Woodpecker.  Two birds counter-called here along the Aker Lake Trail.  The drum was sound recorded, which is very quick and short.
